question_text: A museum visitor looks at a painting where the artist has mixed blue paint and yellow paint together on the canvas. Separately, in a theatre, a lighting technician shines a blue spotlight and a yellow spotlight onto the same white wall. Explain why the colours produced in each situation are different.
correct_answer: The painting shows subtractive colour mixing: blue and yellow pigments absorb (subtract) certain wavelengths, leaving mainly green light to be reflected, so we see green. The theatre shows additive colour mixing: blue and yellow light waves combine in the eye, producing white light (or cyan/light blue), because blue and yellow light together contain most wavelengths of visible light.
explanation: Paint pigments work by subtractive mixing—they absorb colours and reflect what's left. Blue + yellow pigment = green (the colours not absorbed). Light sources use additive mixing—all the light waves reach your eye together. Blue light + yellow light contains enough wavelengths to appear white or very light. This is a key difference between how pigments and light create colour.
